topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rejection email sat accusingly on the screen, a stark white rectangle mocking the effort I’d poured into those essays. I stared at the blinking cursor, my jaw tight, the muscles in my neck corded with tension. Every time I reread the rejection notice—*‘We regret to inform you that your application was not selected for this scholarship’*—a familiar, heavy slump settled into my shoulders. I didn't move from my chair, just let my gaze drift over the cluttered desk, the pile of discarded notes seeming to amplify the dull ache behind my eyes. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped me, tasting like dust. It wasn't anger; it was just a profound lack of energy, a heavy inertia that made even breathing feel like a monumental task. I finally pushed my laptop away, the sound a small, sharp click in the quiet room, and leaned back, letting my head fall against the cool wood of the chair back.

[story 2]

I traced the lines of the denial letter with a fingertip, the paper feeling flimsy and unreal beneath my touch. My hands were cold, clammy, and I kept flexing my fingers, a nervous habit that served only to highlight the hollowness in my chest. I didn't bother trying to delete the email; it was already burned into my focus. My gaze swept across the room—the posters on the wall, the half-finished sketchpad—as if searching for an escape route that simply didn't exist. A low murmur escaped my throat when I finally spoke, the sound raspy and devoid of inflection. "Well," I managed, the word thin and brittle, "that's that, then." I picked up a pen, not to write, but just to turn it over and over in my palm, the repetitive motion a small, self-soothing ritual against the mounting weight of disappointment.

[story 3]

The fluorescent light hummed overhead, an irritating, persistent drone that matched the rhythm of my internal dissatisfaction. I picked at a loose thread on my sweater, the fabric snagging slightly, and watched the tiny piece of unraveling with intense concentration. My posture was closed off; my arms were crossed tightly over my chest, creating a barrier between me and the world. When I finally looked up at the screen displaying the negative decision, my eyes felt strangely flat, unreactive. There was no immediate burst of tears, no shouting—just a deep, pervasive stillness. I shifted my weight from one foot to the other, a barely perceptible movement, before settling back down. The silence in the room seemed to press in, thick and suffocating, mirroring the heavy feeling that had settled permanently into my core.
